pattern: Each day's harvest is a batch with its own expiry day; always eating from the batch that expires soonest (a min-heap keyed by expiry day) is the only way to avoid wasting apples that could still be eaten, and you keep eating on empty-growth days as long as any batch is still fresh.
hints: Think of every day's crop as a (expiryDay, count) batch. On each day, among all batches still fresh, eating from the one that expires soonest is always at least as good as eating from any other.

Use a min-heap of [expiryDay, count] batches. Each day: push today's batch (if it grew any apples), pop and discard any batches at the front whose expiryDay is already <= today, then eat one from the front batch if one remains and decrement its count (removing it if it hits zero).
Keep simulating days past the array's length (with zero new growth) as long as the heap is non-empty, since old batches can still have unexpired apples; stop the moment the heap is empty.

edge_inputs.description: statement example one, five days of varied harvests
statement example two, gaps with no growth
single day, apples that last many days but you can only eat one a day
a huge batch that expires almost immediately gets wasted if not prioritized
all zero-growth days, nothing to eat
every batch has exactly one apple, one-day shelf life, all wasted
two batches expiring on the same day, only one can be finished

stress.description: randomized growth and shelf-life days including zero-growth gaps
